evals.prompt: We're a $12M brand and Segment is quoting us $80k/year. Worth it?
evals.expected_output: Walks the four-archetype grid. At $12M with no warehouse-first stack named, recommends warehouse + reverse-ETL (Hightouch or Census) as the lower-cost path that scales. Calls out Segment as over-spec at this stage unless the brand has 4+ activation surfaces and a marketing-only team. Names the conditions that would flip the recommendation toward packaged CDP.
assertions: Applies the four-archetype decision grid
Recommends warehouse-first as the default at $12M
Names Hightouch or Census as the activation path
Calls out the disqualifying conditions for packaged CDP
Sizes activation surfaces before recommending spend
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move

evals.prompt: Our customer record is a mess — same person shows up as three different records in Klaviyo, two in Gorgias, and the order on Shopify doesn't tie back. Do we need a CDP?
evals.expected_output: Identifies a Level 1 identity-stitching problem solvable with deterministic email + phone matching, not a CDP per se. Walks through whether a warehouse + reverse-ETL stack solves it, vs whether a packaged CDP is justified. Pulls first-touch attribution to size the cross-channel pain.
assertions: Diagnoses the identity-resolution level required
Names Level 1 deterministic stitching as sufficient
Calls modern.attribution.first_touch to size cross-channel pain
Distinguishes the identity fix from the full CDP buy
Recommends the smallest-spend path that solves the problem
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move

evals.prompt: We're about to instrument events on our new site. What schema should we use?
evals.expected_output: Walks the event-schema discipline framework. Verb-Object past-tense naming, mandatory properties per event (customer_id, session_id, timestamp, source), consistent case convention, 15-40 named events with properties for variants. Calls out the irreversibility of schema sprawl and version the schema. References the 20-event starter schema.
assertions: Names the verb-object past-tense convention
Lists mandatory properties per event
Warns against schema sprawl past 40 events
Recommends versioning the schema
References the 20-event starter set
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move

evals.prompt: GDPR keeps coming up in our compliance review. What does our CDP need to do?
evals.expected_output: Walks the five compliance pillars: right-to-deletion within 30 days end-to-end, data minimization, retention windows (7yr purchase / 24mo behavioral / 13mo anonymous), consent state per jurisdiction respected by activation surfaces, cross-border transfer with SCCs or EU residency. Calls out the audit posture rule: if right-to-deletion cannot be explained in three sentences the CDP is not production-ready.
assertions: Lists the five compliance pillars
Names the 30-day right-to-deletion requirement
Names typical retention windows by data type
Notes consent state must be respected per jurisdiction by activation
Names SCCs or EU residency for cross-border data
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move

evals.prompt: If we get a CDP, where does the biggest payoff come from?
evals.expected_output: Walks the activation-surface lift hierarchy. Email lifecycle and Meta lookalike suppression-seed deliver the largest measurable lift per dollar. Onsite personalization is highest-effort, lower-lift. Paid search audience layering is marginal. Recommends activating on 2-3 surfaces, not 7. Pulls cohort LTV to size the personalization payoff.
assertions: Ranks email and Meta suppression-seed at the top of the lift hierarchy
Names onsite personalization as high-effort lower-lift
Calls modern.retention.cohort_ltv to size the payoff
Recommends 2-3 activation surfaces, not all of them
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move

evals.prompt: We're $45M revenue with five channels each above 10% and no CDP. What should we do?
evals.expected_output: Recommends moving off platform-native audiences immediately. With five channels above 10% the identity-stitching pain is structural and gets worse with scale. Pulls revenue by channel to confirm distribution. Names packaged CDP (Segment, Rudderstack, mParticle) or composable warehouse stack depending on engineering headcount.
assertions: Calls modern.sales.by_channel to confirm channel distribution
Calls out platform-native audiences as broken at this revenue and channel count
Names packaged or composable CDP as the two real choices
Conditions the choice on engineering headcount
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move

evals.prompt: A vendor is pitching us probabilistic identity stitching across devices. Do we need it?
evals.expected_output: Identifies Level 3 probabilistic stitching as usually an over-sell for ecomm. Most brands operate at Level 1-2. Asks whether the operator has a named use case that fails without probabilistic stitching (cross-device anonymous attribution is the most common). If not, declines the upsell and recommends Level 2 deterministic + login-based stitching.
assertions: Names probabilistic as Level 3 and typically over-spec for ecomm
Asks for the use case that requires it
Recommends Level 1-2 deterministic stitching as the floor
Declines the vendor upsell absent a named justifying use case
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move
